note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, in spoken order, split at the script's break tags and sentence boundaries. Block windows are each block's measured onset in the joined master: the Σ(audio+0.4) formula predicts 197.923s but the ffprobed master is 197.486s (0.44s off, past the ±0.3s gate — each per-block mp3 carries encoder padding that accumulates), so each block start is the FFT cross-correlation onset of its sNN.mp3 against the joined master (8 kHz mono PCM); measured inter-block gaps are a consistent 0.40s. Windows are contiguous (endS = next onset). Final end = ffprobed master duration 197.486s.

phrases.text: This is where the money rules live.
Open Settings, then the Payments and Finance group.
Nine pages, one list.
Between them they decide how a payment is taken, how a price gets adjusted,
how tax is set, and how a sale lands in accounting.
Learn to spot them, because most billing questions end on one of these.
Start at Payment Types.
This is your tender list, the payment methods the register accepts.
Three flags do the real work.
Locked marks the system types you can't delete.
Require Reference forces a number at the register.
And Show as Bank Drop decides what counts toward the drawer drop.
Now Discounts.
The panels up top read the file at a glance:
how many are active, how they split between dollars off and percent off.
But the row that answers questions is Amount, next to Discount Type.
That pair tells you fast whether a discount takes three dollars off or ten percent.
Open one discount and it behaves like a service.
A detail view, an Active Locations tab, the sites where it's live,
and per-location overrides underneath.
That's how one discount is three dollars off at one wash and two at another.
We couldn't pull this exact screen from today's capture,
so lean on the Services lesson as your reference until we grab it live.
Look, don't save.
Discount Types is the short list behind that column.
Four categories, nothing more.
Whatever shows under Discount Type on the list you just saw is filed from here.
Surcharges are the mirror of a discount.
Same idea, opposite sign.
An added fee, with an amount, a code, and a taxable flag
that decides whether tax rides on top of the fee.
Surcharge Types carries one flag worth knowing.
Disallow Discounts.
Switch it on, and a discount can't stack on a ticket that already has this surcharge.
That single control settles a lot of pricing arguments.
Payout Percentage is a band table.
Each row is a range, a start and an end, with a percentage inside it.
A value drops into whichever band it lands in.
Tiered, not flat.
Tax Rates feeds the rest of the platform.
Every rate here is what a service reaches for in its two tax slots.
So when a wash rings up the wrong tax, don't start at the service.
Start here, and check which rate is the default.
Down to accounting.
General Ledger Codes.
One small catch worth remembering:
the sidebar says General Ledger Codes, the page header says G-L Codes.
Same page.
These codes are what your exports post against,
and a wrong one here is a classic month-end headache.
G-L Account Codes is the partner list.
Same three columns, same shape.
Here's the split: G-L Codes name the ledger lines,
and G-L Account Codes tie them to accounts for the export.
The two work as a pair.
That's the whole group.
Nine pages, one sidebar.
Tender in Payment Types.
Price changes in Discounts and Surcharges.
Tiered payouts, tax, and the two ledger lists.
When a billing question comes in, you already know which page owns the answer.
And you got there without touching a single setting.
